title: Ven Pongal

subs.name: toor-dal
subs.notes: slightly more earthy flavour; reduce water by half a cup as toor dal absorbs less

subs.name: coconut oil
subs.notes: makes it dairy-free; use 2 tbsp instead of 3 — coconut oil is richer than ghee in mouthfeel

subs.name: peanuts (roasted or fried)
subs.notes: more budget-friendly; add with the tempering

steps.text: Wash the rice and moong dal together in two changes of water. Dry-roast the moong dal in a dry pressure cooker base on medium flame, stirring, until it turns light golden and smells nutty — about 2 minutes. This step deepens flavour.

Add the washed rice, 4 cups water and salt to the cooker. Pressure cook on medium-high flame for 4–5 whistles until the rice and dal are very soft and almost dissolving into each other. Let the pressure release naturally.
Open the cooker and stir well — the mixture should be loose and creamy, like thick oatmeal porridge. If it is too thick, add half a cup of hot water and mix on low flame.

Heat ghee in a small tempering pan or ladle. Fry the cashews until golden, then remove and set aside. In the same ghee, add cumin seeds and let them crackle. Add curry leaves, ginger, asafoetida and cracked black pepper and fry for 45 seconds until fragrant.
Pour the entire ghee tempering over the pongal and fold it through. Taste for salt — pongal needs more salt than you expect.
Scatter the fried cashews on top. Serve hot with coconut chutney and sambar. Ven pongal thickens quickly as it cools; reheat with a splash of water.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: ghee → coconut oil (makes it dairy-free; use 2 tbsp instead of 3 — coconut oil is richer than ghee in mouthfeel); cashew → peanuts (roasted or fried) (more budget-friendly; add with the tempering); moong-dal → toor-dal (slightly more earthy flavour; reduce water by half a cup as toor dal absorbs less)
